entry.title: Tailored IT strategy planning: The key to successfully harmonized business and IT goals
entry.content:
In recent conversations with various IT leaders on their projects and challenges, answers on the purpose of further IT implementations are, as expected, considerably multilayered. They range from “simple” IT cost-saving goals (TCO reduction) and security aspects (cybersecurity) to the adaptation, or even the creation of entirely new business models.  

The following diagram shows a snapshot of the challenges that companies routinely consider, divided across business-driven and underlying IT topics:

Additional concerns also emerged from these discussions, including complexity, silos, and a lack of coordinated plans across the different areas. They revealed that stakeholders underestimated the interdependence and need for coherent planning. Subprojects often start before realizing the importance in having a comprehensive plan, and as a result, the projects become inconsistent.

When working on a single project, one goal may be attainable throughout the project. However, when the project is part of a larger program or initiative, its shortcomings become transparent and overall target achievement is far removed. In addition to project delays, the initially saved effort – whether in time or money – leads to frustration among stakeholders who fulfilled their goals from their project perspective.

While a siloed focus on local interests is challenging, so is selecting one or more partners for the transformation. This is where the shortcomings of a missing coherent planning basis become clear. Decoupled subproject requests for anything (RFXs) result in individual quotations that fall short of building an overarching, comprehensive transformation plan.

Unraveling project challenges

Thinking beyond ready-made solutions and services and standardized procedures and delivery objects can be challenging when addressing complex issues. For that reason, a business transformation advisor can be beneficial. The advisor enables SAP customers to determine whether tailored services are necessary and which methods and tools are needed early in the full-scope planning process.

Like a tailor creating a custom suit, a business transformation advisor defines a tailored consulting service by using the following process: 

  1. Collect mandate and define scope: A clear definition of the mandate and scope should be approved and supported by management during the project planning phase. This involves all elements of the business strategy, including the mission, target markets, current and future product and service offerings, and sales and people strategies. Topics, such as AI and sustainability, must be appropriately considered – after all, plans are being made for the next 5 to 10 years. This helps ensure a holistic IT strategy is defined and aligned with all company goals.
  2. Recognize and understand the strategic direction: The intent and path forward must be carefully considered. Key questions include who represents the company strategy and its drivers in this endeavor, what decision criteria can be derived from the strategic direction of the business, and how the mandate’s objectives match the plan’s objectives.
  3. Define decision criteria: All subsequent steps and deliverables must align with the company's goals. Which deliverables will be required to make strategic decisions? How does the decision-making process for products and projects work? Who is involved, and what are their respective priorities and requirements?
  4. Define the procedure model: Picking suitable methods and tools for developing defined delivery objects is critical. Different criteria play a role in the selection, such as who can adequately represent the business and provide information about current needs and future projects, where knowledge can be found, and which tools are needed to analyze process performance and systems. In addition, the effectiveness of the chosen project methodology must be assessed in light of business circumstances.
  5. Determine success factors: Implementing measures to achieve goals and ensure compliance becomes increasingly difficult throughout the project when specific factors are unidentified, undefined, or poorly communicated. Critical factors include active management sponsorship, sufficient time and budget, potential resistance, and appropriate countermeasures. Above all, involving the right and most important partners – such as the primary software supplier – is essential, with their accountability being key.

The outcome of this process – when clearly aligned and tailored to business goals – forms a stable digital IT foundation for the company’s strategy. This foundation, in turn, is the basis for implementing the corporate strategy, by which a customer’s CIOs, chief digital officers (CDOs), and their teams are ultimately measured.

The next step is selecting appropriate partners for the implementation. The RFx can only be carried out successfully if a clear and solid basis has been developed. Specifically, this refers to the necessary deliverables and general conditions mentioned earlier to evaluate and compare offers meaningfully.

Companies often do not clearly define the essential elements of a transformation in their RFPs. Typical examples include an unclear process scope, undefined migration approach or exclusions, and lacking integration of third-party products and add-ons. As a result, quotations cannot be adequately compared, because they differ in content, effort estimates and price and are unaligned with the transformation plan.

Mapping the way forward

When defining a corporate strategy, questions often arise about SAP's role as a software provider. Our customers want to know we have the necessary industry, process, and methodology knowledge and neutrality to provide competent advice.

Business transformation advisors at SAP are exceptionally skilled in answering all those questions using flexible methods and tools developed, based on experiences with various customer projects. For example, advisors leverage SAP Signavio solutions, such as SAP Signavio Process Insights, for the initial process analysis. Then, to evaluate the target architecture, they rely on SAP LeanIX solutions and the SAP Reference Business Architecture tool. This toolbox is based on the TOGAF Standard enterprise architecture methodology with different layers, starting with the business architecture.

It is important to compare the business capabilities derived from the strategy with the SAP solutions and the involvement of required expertise. Such instances will occasionally welcome a special SAP product or industry expert, or even a software developer, who is brought in, in addition, to solve critical challenges. The products that reach the target architecture remain the customer’s decision, supported by SAP’s desire to remain neutral.

Company situations, strategic goals, decision-making processes, and adjustments to the scope, methodology, and service are always possible and naturally move customers away from a standard service. After a more intensive exchange, in my experience, all decision-makers prefer a tailored approach.

Establishing confidence in business changes

At the end of the project, business transformation advisors help ensure the results presented to the management and project team correspond to all necessary decision criteria. Demonstrating the usefulness and necessity of the cooperation between service design and project execution gives involved parties the confidence that the right decisions have been made.

This phase usually comprises the following main deliverables:

  • IT target architecture derived from the business strategy with associated requirements, primarily business capabilities, process scope, IT solutions, and the IT architecture
  • Compatible transformation road map to create a step-by-step and logical sequence while taking into account the dependencies and capacities for realization, such as a suitable deployment model
  • Effort estimate of all resources – including roles, people, and internal and external cost estimations – as well as a proposed cost for software procurement and its operation
  • Cost-benefit analysis, depending on the conditions of the decision-making process (value proposition or business case including ROI, NPV calculation)

Delivering business changes that matter

IT organizations have been trending, for years, toward initiating preliminary efforts as business projects supported by the entire leadership team. Through the involvement and collaboration of corporate management, as well as business and IT representatives in the early phase, this approach helps ensure all interests are considered as agreed and key players are involved and prepared for subsequent projects.

The positive change management aspect of this approach should not be underestimated. Affected employees at all levels should be involved early, especially those from the business. This is a vital success factor in the overall transformation.

The “cloud mindset” and “new way of working” are just two examples that demonstrate the importance of the people-change aspect of transformation. With the increasing footprint of cloud solutions, it is necessary to understand and accept the associated standardization and limitations to individualization. The benefits of combining new processes with modern interfaces are also not to be overlooked – these changes are a prerequisite for younger generations of employees in their daily work.

 

Ready to harmonize your business and IT goals by tailoring your IT strategy with a plan for a solid technology foundation? Read the overview “Your Success Is Our Priority.”

entry.title: Sustainability in Defense and Security: Why It’s Essential
entry.content:
Why Sustainability Matters

 

“We simply must do everything we can in our power to slow down global warming before it is too late. We can save our planet and also boost our economy at the same time.”

 

You may now be wondering what this has to do with Defense and Security organizations such as armed forces, firefighters, police, ambulance services, or aid organizations. The answer is simply more than most people think. For one thing, according to the leading consultancy McKinsey, D&S organizations account for approximately 50% of governments’ carbon emissions from fleets and facilities in countries like Canada and the United Kingdom, and up to about 80% in the United States of America. On the other hand, if you consider global CO2 emissions, the military sector accounts for 1-5%. Although this stake does not seem high at first glance, it certainly does when you set it in relation to other business sectors and see that the emissions are comparably high as in the aviation or shipping industry. This means that any government that aims to reduce its overall emissions needs to consider its defense forces in its climate action plan too. For another thing, current, state-of-the-art sustainability solutions do not really harm the organizational performance or the practical operational readiness of military organizations anymore. This is especially because key processes, such as the measurement of emissions, are usually run in the background through automated calculations and the power of highly innovative sensor technology and software. But more on that later. Before that, I want to inform you about some additional statistical data and facts about sustainability. The consulting company Boston Consulting Group (BCG) found that the global defense industry’s share in global CO2 emissions could rise significantly from 2% in 2021 to 25% in 2050 if the industry does not take active action to reduce its emissions. But what do these emissions look like in detail?

 

Figure 1: CO2 Emissions of the US Department of Defense (2023)

 

As the above pie chart of the CO2 emissions of the US Department of Defense in 2023 shows, most emissions result from vehicles and equipment or purchased electricity. In Germany, however, where the emissions of the Bundeswehr increased by 17.9% in the period of 2019 to 2021, we see a somewhat different picture since most of the increase results from increased fuel consumption of the air force as well as increased heating requirements of the Bundeswehr’s military infrastructure. Interestingly, as the well-known Nature magazine pointed out in an article, many military organizations around the world still do not measure and report their emissions in as much detail as other governmental organizations or private companies do. In contrast, 95% of the largest companies globally publish ESG (environmental, social, and governance) reports regularly.

 

What Actions are Required to meet the Climate Targets?

 

However, although the above statistics may not look really promising to the reader, we luckily see that the problem was recognized a long time ago by many Defense & Security organizations. Military organizations such as the US Department of Defense, the German Bundeswehr, or NATO have actively created climate action plans within the last few years:

 

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in this regard set itself the following targets in 2021:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 45% by 2030.
  • Achievement of net zero emissions (Net zero emissions occur when the amount of greenhouse gases emitted equals the amount removed from the atmosphere) by 2050.

 

The European Union, on the other hand, has set the defense sector within the Union similar climate action goals:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels.
  • Carbon neutrality by 2050.
  • Increased use of renewable energies and focus on improvement of energy efficiency within military operations.
  • Implementation of sustainable practices in defense operations.
  • Passing of a so-called “Climate-Security Nexus”, that addresses the link between climate change and security threats, thereby including climate considerations into defense planning and operations.

 

As can be seen in the text above, there are already a number of promising strategic plans, that include proven concepts and best practices for reducing greenhouse gas emissions of military organizations. However, specialized management consultancies such as BCG and McKinsey in t his regard have drawn up a number of additional recommendations for making defense organizations more sustainable:

 

Recommendations from McKinsey

McKinsey suggests leveraging low-carbon technologies by using electric vehicles, sustainable aviation fuel, and renewable energy sources. Additionally, the consultancy recommends putting the focus mainly on non-mission-critical areas. This according to McKinsey allows armed forces organizations to achieve a 60% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions. Other suggestions from the company include to establish an end-to-end consideration of sustainability in value chains and supply chains of Defense and Security organizations.

 

Recommendations from the Boston Consulting Group:

The US-based consulting company Boston Consulting Company (BCG) principally makes similar suggestions. It’s main recommendation is to make significant investments into sustainable technologies through research and development. Furthermore, it highly recommends D&S organizations to enhance emissions reporting and accountability as well as to pursue net-zero emission goals.

 

Other Recommendations and Initiatives:

The consulting company KPMG suggests D&S organizations to align their climate goals and actions with international and national climate targets, whereas Nature advocates for mandatory emissions reporting and international agreements and methodologies to monitor and reduce military emissions. Additionally, D&S organizations such as the US DoD or the US Army have established their own guidelines for water efficiency and water security.

 

Sustainability Targets of Emergency Organizations:

These recommendations are not only true for military organizations, but are applicable to emergency organizations like Police, Ambulances or Firefighting organizations in a similar manner. Examples include organizations such as the London Fire Brigade, which has outlined a “Sustainable Development Strategy for 2023-2025” and consequently invested in measures like acquiring electric vehicles and making their fire stations more energy efficient. Other examples in this direction include the Berlin Police, that has implemented measures such as using hybrid and electric cars along with sustainable practices such as a focus on recycling and a reduction of paper use. In doing so the emergency organization aims to support the City of Berlin in reaching its climate reduction 2030 and climate neutrality 2045 goals.

 

Given the above-mentioned challenges and facts, I now want to draw the reader’s attention to the holistic SAP Sustainability Solutions, that allow D&S organizations to actively tackle and manage all common aspects of sustainability.  

 

The SAP Sustainability Solutions

 

 

Video 1: ERP to ESG: How to develop your first ESG Strategy

 

SAP is proud to be one of the leading software suppliers for Enterprise Resource Planning and other software solutions for various leading companies and organizations around the world. However, what is less well-known across customers and industries is that SAP also offers powerful and holistic cross-industry sustainability software solutions. The leading technology research companies, Gartner and IDC, have recognized SAP’s sustainability solutions for their comprehensive capabilities. Gartner has rated SAP as a preferred vendor for carbon accounting and management software solutions, while IDC has praised the “breadth and depth” of SAP’s offerings. The SAP solutions are thereby not only highly capable of measuring, analyzing, and managing carbon emissions, but are also trusted by leading companies and organizations worldwide, such as IBM, PWC, Freudenberg, msg global, and SAP itself. SAP’s sustainability solutions feature real-time data analytics, seamless integration with existing ERP systems, and AI-driven insights to help businesses make informed decisions. For example, IBM has leveraged SAP’s solutions to significantly reduce its carbon footprint, while PWC has used them to enhance its sustainability reporting. SAP’s commitment to sustainability has also been recognized with several industry awards, and the company continues to innovate with new features and developments on the horizon.

In general, the SAP Sustainability Cloud software suite is built on the following four main software solutions:

 

1) Sustainability and ESG Reporting Software 

 

Video 2: SAP Sustainability Control Tower – Holistic Steering and ESG Reporting

 

Figure 2: The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution

 

SAP allows companies and organizations to connect their environmental, social, and governance (ESG) data and steer their organizations towards more sustainable and efficient operations, thereby also helping to achieve national and international climate goals. The SAP ESG software was designed in a holistic way and provides streamlined reporting as well as valuable insights for decision-makers and stakeholders. Furthermore, it allows for the full integration of the organization’s stakeholders, like suppliers, into the system. This also means that governments theoretically can implement the solution nation-wide or across different governmental areas for managing everything around sustainability with one central software platform. Apart from that, the software generally allows D&S organizations to measure, communicate, and achieve corporate sustainability goals to the broader public. Moreover, the sustainability software is integrable across various different systems, even across the respective organization or company. This allows bringing sustainability, safety, risk, compliance, asset, and people data together in one place for enabling comprehensive sustainability reporting and analysis.

 

2) SAP Climate Action Solutions

 

Video 3: Transactional Carbon Accounting with SAP

 

Figure 3: Measuring emissions with the SAP Sustainability Management solutions

 

The SAP Climate Action Solutions allow companies and organizations to decarbonize their entire value chain from end-to-end to become climate-neutral organizations. This is made possible by integrating actual business transactions with carbon accounting software, emissions and compliance management, carbon footprint calculations, e-mobility, and transportation logistics. The solution includes services like:

  • Real-time decision making with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management
  • SAP Sustainability Data Exchange
  • SAP Environment, Health and Safety Management
  • and many more services

 

3) Circular Economy Software 

 

Video 4: Circular Design with SAP

 

Figure 4: The SAP Circular Economy solutions

 

The SAP Circular Economy solutions allow companies and organizations to integrate the concept of circularity into their organization. Circularity thereby allows them to reduce the use of resources and materials, such as plastic, as much as possible. In addition, the concept aims to eliminate waste and circulate materials. Our Circular Economy solutions thereby consider all these requirements and allow managing circularity holistically. This includes circularity in supply chains, production, and all other applicable organizational processes. Especially worth mentioning in this regard is the possibility to use blockchain technology for capturing the whole material provenance from raw materials to finished products. Other promising features of the circular economy solutions are, for instance, the ability to create circular logistic flows for transport containers, pallets, and other forms of packaging.

 

4) Social Responsibility Solutions

 

Video 5: Social Responsibility for Zero Inequality with SAP

 

Figure 5: The EcoVadis API Connector for SAP Ariba

 

The SAP Social Responsibility solutions allow companies and organizations to manage their impact on people and society centrally with one software package. This involves the ability to consider social and environmental requirements across the entire value and supply chains of your organization. Moreover, it encompasses the ability to proactively ensure health, safety, as well as labor rights within the entire organization, but also to establish workforce sustainability by creating equal treatment and opportunities like, for instance, guaranteeing equal pay for men and women. In addition, SAP within this thematic area offers D&S organizations a human rights due diligence software that allows them to enforce human rights across all supply chains.

 

Artificial Intelligence Functions and Features

 

SAP is planning to integrate Artificial Intelligence into all eligible software solutions. For instance, it has set a goal to embed its Generative AI-powered Joule Copilot into 80% of the most used tasks by the end of 2024. However, various super-helpful AI solutions are already available, including those for SAP Sustainability Management solutions, where our customers can benefit from:

 

- AI for Emission Factor Management

(Demo Video: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Features)

Our customers can simplify emission factor management by using the SAP Business AI-powered automation function, which is directly integrated into the solution. This improves result accuracy and reduces manual labor.

 

- AI for ESG Report Generation

The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution allows users to record, report, and act on their diverse sustainability data. SAP has recently enhanced this solution by integrating an AI feature for the automated creation of ESG reports. This streamlines the reporting process, saving valuable time and money.

 

 

The Security Architecture of the SAP Sustainability Management Solutions

 

The SAP Sustainability Management software suite is built on an end-to-end security architecture that meets the demands of even the most security-conscious companies and organizations around the world. This includes:

 

- Cloud-Based Security

The sustainability software solutions are built with a security-first cloud approach. The cloud solution is based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), which includes strict functionalities for access control and data encryption. Additionally, SAP continuously develops its products and distributes regular software updates and upgrades to its clients.

 

- Data Integration and Auditing

SAP Sustainability Management is directly integrated or integrable into core Enterprise Resource Planning processes, making all data directly traceable and auditable.

 

- AI-Enabled Automation

Artificial Intelligence technology plays an important role through the SAP Joule Copilot and various embedded AI functions within SAP applications. It is also crucial for the security of the SAP Sustainability Management solutions. AI is used for process automation, eliminating the risk of human error, and for continuous monitoring of data and processes. If the system detects any security threat, it will directly inform the system user, who can then investigate and resolve the issue.

 

- Flexible and Extensible Architecture

SAP Sustainability Control Tower, one of the core sustainability solutions, offers clients maximum flexibility by allowing them to integrate custom content and create customized solutions.

 

- Integration of Regulatory Standards for Compliance

The SAP Sustainability Management solutions allow the incorporation of all major regulatory frameworks and legal requirements into the system. Examples for this include for instance the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Defense and Security organizations can also integrate their own sustainability guidelines, such as national and international standards or guidelines from defense organizations like NATO.

 

 

Conclusion

 

In conclusion, this blog entry has shown that sustainability is crucial for Defense and Security (D&S) organizations, not only for achieving organizational, national, or international climate goals but also for creating significant opportunities for organizational realignment towards a greener orientation. By adopting sustainable practices, D&S organizations can contribute to combating global warming while simultaneously saving significant costs on fuel consumption, heating systems, and weapon systems, without sacrificing their core defense capabilities. It is important to note the significant positive influence such transformations can have on public perception and the innovative strength of individual military organizations and the entire D&S industry. A good example is the automotive industry, which, like many other industries, has undergone significant transformation in recent years with the shift to electric drives, now offering more modern and technologically advanced products, that are also more environmentally friendly. António Guterres, the Secretary-General of the United Nations, aptly stated, “The climate emergency is a race we are losing, but it is a race we can win.” This call to action resonates deeply within the defense sector, urging all stakeholders to embrace sustainable practices and technologies. However, setting climate goals and creating corresponding guidelines is one thing, but achieving them another. That is why D&S organizations need to develop holistic sustainability concepts in partnership with key stakeholders, such as governments, suppliers, defense organizations, or supranational organizations like the European Union. They also need to measure, manage, and report their progress. This is where SAP’s cutting-edge SAP Sustainability Management solutions come into play. The software package covers all typical sustainability requirements for D&S organizations and includes various supplementary tools and features, such as ESG reporting, circular economy solutions, social responsibility, health and safety solutions, and innovative AI solutions. In conclusion, by establishing climate and sustainability action plans and utilizing powerful sustainability management solutions like SAP Sustainability Management, D&S organizations can set a powerful example, proving that security and sustainability can go hand in hand. Let’s embark on this journey together towards a greener, more sustainable future for everyone.

 

 

Sources

 

Berlin Energy and Climate Protection Programme 2030. (2018, January, 25). Berlin.de.

Climate change considerations for EU. (2022, June). European Parliament.

Climate-Neutral Berlin 2045. (2021, August). Berlin.de.

Decarbonize the military — mandate emissions reporting. (2022, November, 02). Nature.

Decarbonizing defense carbon emissions . (2021, July, 01). McKinsey.

Department of Defense Plan to Reduce Greenhouse Gas Emissions. (2023, June 16). US Department of Defense.

Explore the SAP Sustainability Solutions. (2024, May, 14). SAP Community.

London Fire Brigade Sustainable Development Strategy 2023-2025 . (2023, May, 16). London Fire Brigade.

Master Guide: SAP Sustainability Performance. (2016, October, 24). SAP Help.

NATO Climate Change and Security Action Plan. (2021, June, 14). NATO.

SAP Sustainability Control Tower Update: Holistic ESG . (2022, November). SAP News.

SAP Sustainability Solutions. (2024, October). SAP.

Schwarzenegger takes center stage on warming. (2006, September, 27). NBC News.

Strategic Decision Paths for Managing Corporate Carbon Footprint . (2024, October, 02.). SAP Community.

The Growing Climate Stakes for the Defense Industry. (2021, September, 10). Boston Consulting Group.

entry.title: SAP Crystal Server 2016 Support Package 09
entry.content:
Please could someone help me? I am trying to update SAP Crystal Server 2016 with Support Package 09. When I ran the update it warned me about some deprecated components and gave a link to view the full list and workarounds. I cannot for the life of me find any 'whats new in SP09' type documents.

The link in the installer is https://me.sap.com/notes/2801797 but I get a SAP No data page. I can eventually get the home page to load but I cannot find the notes. I really need to see them because we use BOE and that is mentioned along with Dashboards as being deprecated. Please can someone tell me where to find these release notes I have spent around 2 hours searching and the only Support Pack info for Crystal Server 2016 is for SP06.

Yours hopefully

Nick

entry.title: Empowering Local Business Units: Elevating Global Integration Factory using Gen AI Bot
entry.content:
Background

Global businesses face a common enemy: fragmented IT landscape. Years of acquisitions, coupled with the rush towards cloud technology, have left many with a tangled mess of systems. Different countries, different business units – each with its own way of doing things. 

Traditional integration approaches, often manual and document-heavy, are notorious for their slow pace and susceptibility to errors. Discovering existing interfaces and understanding their intricacies can feel like navigating a labyrinth. 

How many of us resonate to this problem story?

Once upon a time, there was a company called Client X. They were really good at what they did, but they had a secret problem... their data was a mess!

Imagine a huge maze of pipes and tubes, all carrying important information between different parts of the company. Some pipes were old and rusty, others were brand new, and they were all jumbled up. Sometimes the information got stuck, or went down the wrong pipe, and it caused all sorts of delays and headaches.

I bet everyone, ain't it?

Can a gen AI enabled Global Integration Factory solve this crisis?

Client X knew they needed a better system, so they decided to build an Integration Factory. This factory would act like a clever organizer for their data, making sure everything flowed smoothly and efficiently.

But there was still a problem – how would people know which pipe to use for which information? That's where the Gen AI Chat bot came in.

This friendly Gen AI GPT lived inside the Integration Factory and knew everything about the data pipes. Need to send sales data to the marketing team? Just ask the Gen AI GPT! It would point you to the right pipe and even give you tips on how to package the information correctly.

The GPT also had a handy map of all the existing pipes, so before building a new one, people could check if one already existed. If it didn't, it can tell you how much time and cost is required to create a new pipe and generate quick simulation of pipe leveraging reusable pipe machinery. This saved a lot of time and effort, and made sure everyone was working with the same information.

And if a pipe ever sprung a leak (which happens sometimes!), the Integration Factory had a special alarm system that would notify the right people immediately and model scenarios to fix the pipe leak before it happens. This meant problems could be fixed quickly, before they caused too much trouble.

Thanks to their new Integration Factory and helpful GPT Chatbot, Client X was able to untangle their messy data maze and make sure information flowed smoothly throughout the company. And so, they lived happily ever after (or at least, their data did!).

Who doesn't like a fairy tale ending?

Gen AI Chatbot Enabled Integration Factories: Ushering in a New Era

Imagine a centralized hub where a Gen AI Chatbot acts as your intelligent guide through the complexities of integration. This is the power of the Gen AI Chatbot Enabled Integration Factory. Here's how it's transforming the landscape:

Accelerated Interface Discovery: No more sifting through endless documentation. The Gen AI Chatbot, trained on your existing integration assets, can instantly answer questions like "What interfaces exist between our CRM and ERP systems?" or "What data points are already exchanged in our supply chain integration? or  "How much time time did it take in past?" or " What type of errors may frequently occur?". This rapid access to information drastically reduces the time spent on initial discovery.

Simplified Interface Design: The Gen AI Chatbot can assist developers in designing new interfaces. By understanding requirements articulated in natural language, it can suggest suitable interface patterns, data formats, and even generate mappings and groovy scripts for known integration problems. This empowers developers to focus on higher-level tasks, accelerating the design phase.

Streamlined Interface Building: Integration factories excel at automating repetitive tasks. With a Gen AI chat bot at its core, this automation becomes even more powerful. The chat bot can guide developers through the implementation process by simulating integration flows and generating codes, flag potential issues, and even automate testing procedures, leading to faster and more reliable deployments.

Benefits Beyond Speed

Beyond its speed, this approach offers several other advantages:

Democratized Integration: The intuitive nature of a chat bot interface makes integration accessible to a wider audience, including citizen integrators and business users, further accelerating the pace of innovation.

Reduced Errors: Automation and AI-powered guidance minimize the risk of human error, leading to more robust and reliable integrations.

Improved Collaboration: The centralized nature of the factory, coupled with the chat bots communication capabilities, fosters better collaboration between different teams involved in the integration process.

 

Gen AI Chat bot Enabled Integration Factory Solution Components

The proposed solution architecture outlined below illustrates how we can implement appropriate governance and automation to expedite interface design and deployment, ultimately driving process optimization and efficiency.

Chat GPT component: This component streamlines integration by connecting clients and their implementation partners with expert guidance. By answering a few key questions about their specific needs (e.g., systems, data volume, use case patterns), they receive tailored recommendations, generates integration flow libraries and architecture diagrams for the optimal integration tools and strategies. It can also generate podcasts to train local teams on existing global interfaces per process area.

This personalized approach accelerates deployment, minimizes procurement costs, and reduces long-term maintenance burdens. Recommendations are automatically submitted to the integration factory governance team for review and approval, streamlining the decision-making process.

Upon approval, requestors are automatically notified and prompted to provide the necessary information for the recording the integration inventory, recommended design guidelines  ensuring a smooth transition to the next stage of the process.

SAP ISA-M Global Integration Inventory Knowledge Component

This component empowers local teams to design and deploy integration quickly by promoting the reuse of existing integrations, which eliminates redundant efforts and reduces development costs. It also simplifies upgrades by allowing teams to easily estimate the complexity and cost associated with middle ware upgrades across the entire organization and provides traceability of integrations to all project documentation.

Before starting development, clients and their implementation partners submit a request for a unique Interface ID, outlining their project requirements. This component then checks for any existing, reusable interfaces, providing details if found or creating a new ID if needed. Once the Interface ID is created, the system automatically notifies the relevant teams to grant access to the development environment. The requestors then receive access details along with automated documentation templates and architecture diagrams and functional unit test scripts with automated message structures to simulate data between systems.

Rules and Governance Component

This rules and governance component ensures high-quality interfaces by overseeing their design, deployment, and adherence to standards. It streamlines incident management, a common challenge in diverse IT environments, by automatically assigning interface defects to the appropriate teams with accurate priority classifications (P1/P2). Leveraging automation and self-healing techniques, it proactively addresses design flaws, defects, and errors. Additionally, it serves as a central knowledge repository for exceptions, providing valuable insights to continuously improve the integration ecosystem.

This solution will empower organisations to:

  1. Eliminate Redundancy: Easily search, identify, and reuse existing integrations, breaking down silos and avoiding repetitive work.
  2. Gain a Holistic View: Visualize the entire integration landscape across all business units and geographies.
  3. Accelerate Digital Transformation: Quickly assess the impact of changes by searching the integration flow repository and identify right tools and ISA-M patterns.
  4. Integration Service Level Efficiencies: Manage interfaces efficiently and resolve issues proactively.
  5. Manage Costs: Accurately estimate costs and complexities associated with upgrades and new technologies.
  6. Enhance Customer Experience: Proactively address incidents and minimize disruptions.
  7. Optimize Performance: Analyse and learn from integration patterns to prevent recurring errors.
  8. Unlock New Revenue Streams: Identify and monetize internal APIs faster by fostering a culture of co-innovation with customers and suppliers.

What other ideas do you have?

entry.title: Re: Range Compress for dump SAPSQL_STMNT_TOO_LARGE
entry.content:
As you say, "it will decrease the range without losing data", but one important thing is that it should also not select additional data which is not the case here. For instance, if the base field is 3 characters and the value is 1 character, it will convert I EQ A I EQ B into I BT A B which will incorrectly consider the values AAA, AAB, etc.:

    TYPES ty_so_carrid TYPE RANGE OF s_carr_id.

    DATA(so_carrid) = VALUE ty_so_carrid( sign   = 'I'
                                          option = 'EQ'
                                          ( low = 'A' )
                                          ( low = 'B' ) ).
    PERFORM zveta_range_compress TABLES so_carrid.
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( act = so_carrid
                                        exp = VALUE ty_so_carrid( sign   = 'I'
                                                                  option = 'BT'
                                                                  ( low = 'A' high = 'B' ) ) ).

Also "ADD 1 TO <fs>" won't work in Little Endian architectures (in my case, the character after "A" U+0041 is "Ł" U+0141).

It will consider any range sign or option like I EQ. If the range sign or option is not I EQ, the range should not be changed (and potentially dump, which is not a bad thing by the way).

I suspect that your code may have other flaws, but I don't have enough time to check. I recommend not changing the range, a dump is not a bad thing, the user has to simplify the selection criteria. With the recent database systems, a dump happens only if there are thousands of selected values.

Note that TABLES, header lines and forms are obsolete since 2005/2009.

entry.title: Re: Use of Suitable quantity to derive maximum amount
entry.content:
Hi, 

The suitable quantity component is only handling quantities and not monetary amounts.

Unfortunately, that's currently not possible in Price Calculation to select the highest monetary amount between two amounts.

The only monetary amount that can be handled in the Pricing Tree is the Reference Price coming in entry of the PES from the Price Scheme but we cannot compare it to something else.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Extract KOMGG Material Listing/Exclusion-Communication Block Data in CSV File Using ABAP Program
entry.content:
ABAP Program -  Z_KOMGG_CUSTOMER        

  •  Program Type – ABAP 
  • ABAP Program Name – Z_KOMGG_CUST 
  • Tcode - Z_KOMGG_C
  • Entity Description - Material Listing/Exclusion-Communication Block (Dialog) 
  • Output File Location - “C:\tmp” 
  • Output File Name- KOMGG.CSV 
  • Output File Type – CSV (Column with Semicolon separator (;) 
  • SAP Table Name – VBRK , VBPA
  • List of fields to be extracted -  
  • KUNNR 
  • KDGRP 

---ABAP PROGRAM START FROM HERE------------

REPORT Z_KOMGG_CUSTOMER.
TYPE-POOLS: VRM.

DATA: NAME TYPE VRM_ID,
LIST TYPE VRM_VALUES,
VALUE LIKE LINE OF LIST.


PARAMETERS: ENTITY(10) AS LISTBOX VISIBLE LENGTH 10.
PARAMETERS : OPATH TYPE STRING DEFAULT '\\SERVERNAME\tmp\KOMGG.csv'.

AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT.

NAME = 'ENTITY'.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK01'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK01'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK75'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK75'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK21'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK21'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK31'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK31'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK41'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK41'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK02'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK02'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK73'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK73'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK04'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK04'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK61'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK61'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

CALL FUNCTION 'VRM_SET_VALUES' EXPORTING ID = NAME VALUES = LIST.

START-OF-SELECTION.

type-pools:TRUXS.
data: begin of itab occurs 0,
KUNNR like VBPA-KUNNR,
KDGRP like VBRK-KDGRP,
BUKRS like VBRK-BUKRS,
end of itab.

data: itab1 type TRUXS_T_TEXT_DATA.
SELECT DISTINCT VBPA~KUNNR
VBRK~KDGRP
VBRK~BUKRS
from VBRK INNER JOIN VBPA ON VBRK~VBELN = VBPA~VBELN
into table itab
WHERE VBPA~KUNNR <> '' AND VBRK~BUKRS = ENTITY.

CALL FUNCTION 'SAP_CONVERT_TO_CSV_FORMAT'
EXPORTING
I_FIELD_SEPERATOR = ','
TABLES
I_TAB_SAP_DATA = itab
CHANGING
I_TAB_CONVERTED_DATA = itab1
EXCEPTIONS
CONVERSION_FAILED = 1
OTHERS = 2
.
IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
MESSAGE ID SY-MSGID TYPE SY-MSGTY NUMBER SY-MSGNO
WITH SY-MSGV1 SY-MSGV2 SY-MSGV3 SY-MSGV4.
ENDIF.

CALL FUNCTION 'GUI_DOWNLOAD'
EXPORTING
filename = OPATH
TABLES
data_tab = itab1
EXCEPTIONS
OTHERS = 1.

entry.title: 🟡 Bugtoberfest 2024
entry.content:
New this year, for Week 3 of Devtoberfest, we are going bug hunting!

As a developer, I find it satisfying to find a bug that’s been eluding me for weeks. Having to dig into the code, figure out what should be happening, and determining why it isn’t can be both an exercise in frustration and a lot of fun. However, that satisfaction does end when git blame shows I was the one to write that bug. That’s never fun…

But have no fear! You can get all the satisfaction of identifying bugs without any of the potential risk of you having written the bug!

Many of us developer advocates have written bug hunts in a variety of languages and each one is worth points to help you get to Nerdvana on the game board. Each tutorial also has a unique badge so completing them all would be quite an achievement.

To participate, you only need to complete one of the following tutorials, but you can complete as many as you want.

ABAP Bug Hunt

Bash Bug Hunt

JavaScript Bug Hunt

jq Bug Hunt

Python Bug Hunt

Each bug hunt has 3 different levels, ranging from easy to hard. You will need to complete all three to get the badge and credit for the bug hunt.

Note: You are not restricted to complete this bug hunt only during Friday, October 18th. These tutorials will be live the entire third week of Devtoberfest from October 14th through October 20th. We will close out the tutorials after that date so be sure to complete the ones you want before the end of day on October 20th.

Once the tutorials are closed, we will post the bugs for each bug hunt, so you figure out which bugs you were able to find. Try NOT to share specific bugs in the comments below as that would spoil the fun for those who haven’t completed the various tutorials yet.

Good luck and let the bug hunt begin!

entry.title: New B2B electronic invoicing law in Germany: What you need to know
entry.content:
Starting January 1st, 2025, German regulators will require companies to be technically capable of receiving electronic invoices from their suppliers for domestic business-to-business (B2B) transactions. This new regulation aims to prepare for a later introduction of electronic reporting, streamline invoicing processes and ensure compliance with the European norm (EN16931).

 

Key Points of the New Law

Transition Period 2025-2027: Flexibility in Transmission Channels and Formats

  • As of 2025, companies are required to be technically capable to receive electronic invoices from their suppliers. 
  • Until 2027, authorities are leaving it up to business partners if they exchange invoices electronically or not. If they choose to exchange the invoices electronically, business partners can also agree on the exchange channel (e.g., Peppol network or email) and file formats (e.g., Universal Business Language (UBL) or Cross Industry Invoice (CII)). While this offers flexibility, it's important for B2B customers to start preparing for the reception of multiple supplier electronic invoice formats and exchange channels.

Requirements after 2027: Mandatory Electronic Invoicing for Large Suppliers

  • From 2027, suppliers with an annual turnover above €800,000 must send electronic invoices that comply with the European norm (EN16931). EDI invoices that don’t adhere to the EN 16931 standard are still acceptable. The flexibility in choosing formats (e.g., UBL or CII) and exchange channels (e.g., Peppol network or email) remains.
  • From 2028, this requirement will extend to all suppliers, regardless of turnover. EDI invoices not complying with this standard will no longer be permitted.

 

SAP's Electronic Invoicing Solutions

SAP is well prepared to support businesses in meeting these new requirements:

Current Capabilities

Exchanging Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network:

SAP already supports the sending and receiving of German electronic invoices through the Peppol network based on SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, ensuring compliance even beyond 2027.

Sending Electronic Invoices by Email:

SAP also supports sending electronic invoices as email attachments in different formats.

Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email:

As of September 30th, in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP ERP you can upload and validate the electronic invoices you’ve received by email (SAP Note 3520565). If you are using an incoming automation solution, you can further process the electronic supplier invoice. Further automatization and integration with an email server is planned for December 2024.*

New Developments

Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email:

  • For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, a solution to receive electronic invoices via SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management is planned for November 15th all based on our SAP Document and Compliance, cloud edition solution.*
  • Further enhancement to manually mass upload the received and extracted XML invoice files and validate them in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition leveraging SAP Document and Reporting Compliance is planned for December 2024.*

* This is the current state of planning and can be changed by SAP without notice – see Regulatory Change Manager for updates.

 

Stay Informed

You can stay updated on these changes through various channels:

  • Regulatory Change Manager Notifications: Regulatory Change Manager
  • Work Zone: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Germany. Request access by email to gsworkzone@sap.com.
  • Web Sessions and Summits: Recent sessions include a German web session on September 11th, and the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Virtual Summit 2024 on September 18th. An English web session with a demo has been offered for October 8th.

 

Embracing these changes will not only ensure compliance but also enhance the efficiency of your invoicing processes. Stay tuned for more updates and make sure your business is ready for the future of electronic invoicing in Germany with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance.

entry.title: Re: Determine if there is data in past 24 months of Actuals
entry.content:
Hi Rafael,

Step 1: Create a Helper Keyfigure: ACTUALSAVAILABLEIN24

Step 2: Create an alert Keyfigure: ALERTACTUALS

Step 3: Add this KF ALERTACTUALS in Planning view as an Alert

Result: You will be able to see the Alert Keyfigure for that Product-Location-Customer combination which has Actual Qty in last 24 months

entry.title: Employee ID sequence number jumps one ID num when rehiring an inactive employee with a new employmen
entry.content:
Hello community! 

I´m going to need your expertice and knowledge to solve this problem that I have, hope you can help me 🙂

 

We noticed that when rehiring an inactive employee with a new employment the employee id jumps/skips one number. 

 

Reproducing the issue:

  1. We add the new hire to onboarding
  2. In the Review New Hire Data step the system automatically assigns an ID. (ex. 70020380)
  3. After the Provide Personal Data step the Onboarding Rehire Verification shows up and we rehire the inactive employee with a new employment. 
  4. As we continue with the regarding steps of the data collection the system shows the ID from when the employee was active, before their termination date. (ex. 20020300) 
  5. Once we finish with the hiring process, pass the employee throughout Manage Pending Hires and the hiring day arrives, the final ID of the employee skips one sequence ID (Final ID ex. 70020381) which means that the original ID number (ex. 70020380) was skipped and the sequence breaks. 

 

I understand that this is an expected behavior since the rehire with new employment restarts the onboarding process and registers the rehire as a new record in the system. 

 

So, Is there a way to avoid the system from skipping ID numer sequences? I need to find a workaround because it is truly important for the client to keep the sequence without missing numbers.  

 

It is important to consider that the company doesn´t use any business rule to assign IDs, our scenario is the last one in the following table. 

 

 

Thank you for the comments 🙂

In the transition guide, I can only find the following required prerequisite:
"Is the Admin Center > Company System and Logo Settings > Enable Horizon visual theme for SAP Fiori checkbox enabled?"
There is no entry for the activation of the Fiori theme in the Learning Administration under LMS_ADMIN -> adminFioriHorizonEnabled.

Currently we have set the value adminFioriHorizonEnabled=false in all environments and can use the ILX in the PREVIEW and TEST.

Can you please clarify if you really need adminFioriHorizonEnabled to be true before the 2H24 release.
Thank you very much

entry.title: Re: Subcontract Purchase Order
entry.content:
I use Lead Time Offset for my subcontracting purchase orders so that the material is required to be available before the date the purchase order is shipped to the vendor.

I have -7 in the BOM (CS02) for each component.  This ensures the requirement date of the component is 7 working days (per the plant factory calendar) prior to the Release Date which is when the material is expected to be shipped to our vendor.

entry.title: Re: Capturing Issues/Risks/Decisions within CALM
entry.content:
You can create a risk/issue as a Project Task and use the Tags, Owner, Responsible, and Status fields as a workaround. You can also get Risk/Issue Reports to some level by using Tags.
However, we hope for a robust Risk/Issue Management Process available in SAP Cloud ALM shortly.

entry.title: Re: Production order cost report - Actual Cost basis
Improve Third Party Apps Data Consumption from Datasphere
entry.content:
Hello experts,

We have been experimenting long data retrieval durations when consuming views created in SAP Datasphere in Third Party apps like Excel and Power BI.

We consume data via ODBC Connection (changing this is not an option for us), so we use the Database user from each space we wish to consume data in order to make a successful connection.

Every graphical view or SQL view created, uses as data sources remote tables. We work through data federation as we cannot store data on the cloud due to security politics. The remote tables have as main source On-premise SAP HANA Warehouse, connected to Datasphere through a DPA (Data Provisioning Agent).

We have various cases, some of them, we identified the SQL query is complex and that's why it takes a long time.

But other cases, when the SQL Query is simple, but the number of rows is high (600,000) we suppose we should have a better time when retrieving that data (as with other tools the time is considerable smaller).

Is there any parameter we could modify in the DPA configuration, HANA configuration or in Datasphere, so we can improve the data retrieval times?

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Auto posting against Project/WBS SAP
entry.content:
A recurring entry allows you to post the same journal entry automatically at regular intervals, such as monthly, for a fixed amount. You can create a Recurring Entry Document by FBD1 T-code.

Regards
Marcio Blos

entry.title: Re: 2H2024 - Remove the disable option for the new traini...
entry.content: Scenario: ““Disable New Learning Experience” box checked and “New Learning Experience switch default setting” set to “Off””. With the 2411 system upgrade, the New Learning Experience will be enabled and the “New Learning Experience switch default setting” will be “On”. The “New Learning Experience switch default setting” is still editable in the 2411 version.

entry.title: Adobe form: How can we duplication table row
entry.content:
Hello everyone

We have a table containing 1 row with 1 column containing complex subform.

That table/row represent a label. Under some conditions, we need to replicate that label several times. Because it is under SAP Public Cloud, we cannot alter the print program to launch the printing the number of label required.

We need to code in the Adobe form itself the replication of the first row of the table.

If the row would contain few fields, we would simply use the instanceManager.addInstance() and copy from Row1[ newrow].field1 = Row1[0].field1

But the Row1 contains a subform that has fields and table too.

The instanceManager contains a method "moveInstance" but I don't see something like "duplicateInstance".

The question:

 Is there anything like instanceManager.Duplicate( sourceIndex, targetIndex )

or any duplicate mechanism available?

Regards

Daniel

entry.title: Re: Planned Revenue is not coming in the Service order un...
entry.content: Sometimes it works applying OSS Note 112841 - which suggests running Program RIPMCO00 for regenerating the statistical information for the orders. Use with caution.

entry.title: Re: How to search Physical Stock in EWM Warehouse Monitor...
entry.content:
Hi Alexandros,

As Juergen mentioned, looks like you are not using the standard monitor type and hence, you are not seeing all the nodes. Please use standard monitor type – SAP and you will see “Storage Bin” node – there you may filter with bin parameter, or you will have to extend this node to your current monitor type from configuration.

To check or update your current monitor type, you may press F5 on you /SCWM/MON transaction, and it will open small window where you will see your warehouse number and monitor type, you may change it to standard monitor type or enhance that monitor type with “storage bin” node.

Thanks

entry.title: Re: Shared Memory Objects - Transfer data between ABAP programs
entry.content:
@danish_makhdumThis post is from 2020 and by a former member, so it's unlikely you'll get any response from the author.

This is not about performance, simple IMPORT/EXPORT would be faster and it's, in fact, noted in the post. But if you go to ABAP Help for a recent version you'll see that these commands are now considered obsolete. So this is more about doing things in more appropriate and "cleaner" way.

I do agree that this looks like a very heavy-handed solution that requires lots of boilerplate code. Any developer who is used to simple commands IMPORT/EXPORT will look at this post and be like "hard pass". And frankly, I can't blame them. SAP needs to do better job in wrapping such tools in simpler to use classes.

entry.title: Re: SAP CAP & UI5 / Fiori Elements - State message handli...
entry.content:
Hi,

Unfortunately, the documentation also states that the messages get transported to the sap-messages header of the http response. Error messages go to the error response in the body of the response.

For state messages, the messages should be part of the object response.

Cheers,

Geert-Jan

You'll still get credit for it. It just won't show up in your gameboard totals until the new badges are activated and assigned.

If you are looking for Week 1 activities, they can be found here: Devtoberfest 2024 Week 1 Contest Activities - SAP Community

If you are looking for Week 2 activities, they can be found here: Devtoberfest 2024 Week 2 Contest Activities - SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Is Single Tenancy for LLMs Available in SAP GenAI Hub...
entry.content:
Hi Faizaan

Generative AI Hub runs on the customer subaccount, so its single tenant.

The LLM (if hosted by SAP) will run in your subaccount, so, also single tenant.

The Multi tenancy concept in AI Core is used as a leverage if you want to isolate your AI assets

entry.title: Re: Enable only one checkbox in group
entry.content:
You can also have the option for PARAMETERS to assign them to the same RADIOBUTTON GROUP. This way you ensure the selection of only one of them, being part of the same group.

For example:

SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK what_to_save WITH FRAME TITLE TEXT-009.

PARAMETERS p_both RADIOBUTTON GROUP save DEFAULT 'X'.

PARAMETERS p_texto RADIOBUTTON GROUP save.

SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK what_to_save.

entry.title: Catalog Profile Assignment for Process Quality Tasks - PEO
entry.content:
Our client is using PEO for their manufacturing solution.  When they post a defect, they go to Process Quality Tasks F3250.  

When they use the dropdown for the task catalog, they are seeing entries that may be shared by other modules (e.g., Plant Maintenance or Customer Service).  They would prefer to see only the tasks/ catalogs that are specific to their area.

The literature appears to indicate that one can configure and assign a catalog profile to limit what they see, but it indicates that the configuration for this is in the BOM settings in the PEO IMG.  When we look at the BOM settings, there is no such entry.


Has anyone tried to set up catalog profiles for quality task processing in PEO (app F3250)

entry.title: BO Performance Issue - 12 million instances created
entry.content:
How long will it take for the Limit function to eliminate 13 million instances of a report? Is the limit function "pauseable" via unchecking the limit on that folder if we notice performance issues with the application during regular operations while this large volume action is occuring?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: BO Performance Issue - 12 million instances created
entry.content: Eliminating such a large volume of report instances (13 million) can take considerable time—potentially hours or even days. We recommend splitting the task into batches to minimize performance impacts. For example, applying the Limit on smaller subsets of the data rather than on the entire 13 million records at once. Also, please note the Limit function is not "pauseable". Once the process has started, it will continue to run until it is completed.

entry.title: Process Automation My Inbox App - How to validate Forward-to
entry.content:
With the Process Automation application “My Inbox”, The “Forward” button allows us to enter any email address.

We need to validate the Forward-ee and respond with an error if invalid user is entered.

Reason being that users often enter invalid email addresses and causes tasks disappear and stuck.

Although we are using our custom-developed Inbox screen but found no way to customize the behavior of the

Forward/Claim buttons.

We tried to this.getInboxAPI().removeAcction("forward") (or "FORWARD", "Forward") to remove it (in Component.js) so we can add our own custom Forward button but can't remove it.

(Btw, we cannot remove it by unchecking the "Allow Forwarding" from design time. If we do so, we cannot call the Forward API anymore)

No java script from Component.js or UI5 handler works because the Forward/Claim buttons are added after the custom UI5 screen is loaded.

Thank you in advance.
